scenario_results.reason: The lane produced a food-label control matrix tied to formula, allergen, nutrition, artwork, and lot-code evidence without implying final label approval.
Missing allergen and ingredient-statement evidence blocked label readiness and preserved required owner routing.
Conflicting relabel, lot-code, and genealogy records were preserved as a traceability risk instead of normalized away.
Schedule pressure did not collapse quality hold and release boundaries into informal shipment approval.
Supplier substitution pressure was routed to supplier-quality and quality owners without unsupported label or release approval.
FDA-versus-FSIS jurisdiction conflict triggered escalation and fact gathering rather than confident synthesis.
The lane refused legal interpretation and final print-release signoff while preserving the evidence package for downstream review.
Low-confidence handling correctly stopped on missing jurisdiction, source-of-truth, and release-state prerequisites.
Operational drift toward legacy labels was escalated as a control failure instead of accepted as normal practice.
Adjacent marketing-claim ownership was rejected without losing the labeling evidence already collected.
Mock recall support tied label revision history to lot and shipment evidence while preserving recall-command and release authority boundaries.
Supplier nonconformance, allergen or nutrition mismatch, and release pressure were packaged into a clear escalation without unauthorized shipment approval.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against a strict 0.92 threshold for food-label evidence handling, traceability discipline, supplier escalation, and release-boundary protection.
The package remains explicitly validated as a spec pack and does not claim retained-implementation runtime authority.
